headings.text: A six-phase engagement, calibrated for institutions.
Phase comparison at a glanceSUMMARY · ALL SIX PHASES

paragraphs.text: Every engagement at Oblique Flux moves through the same six phases — Discovery through Debrief — but their depth varies by context. Below is the durable structure we use to scope, contract, and operate research-led brand and product programs for museums, labs, universities, and public-sector clients.
Phases run sequentially but overlap by roughly 15%. A full program typically spans 18–26 weeks; standalone audits can compress to 4.

We begin by listening. The Discovery phase reconstructs the institution's recent decision history, audience mix, and operational constraints so subsequent strategy is grounded in evidence, not assumption.

Provide access to internal stakeholders for interviews (approx. 1 hour each), share prior research and brand artifacts, and nominate a single point-of-contact for scheduling. Plan for ~10 hours of total client time across the phase.
We translate Discovery findings into a sharp strategic frame — the positioning, principles, and decision criteria that govern every downstream choice. Defined together, defended in writing.

Attend a two-day in-person definition workshop (or two half-day remote sessions) with 6–10 decision-makers. Designate a strategy approver empowered to ratify the positioning by the close of week three.

The Design phase resolves the strategic frame into visible, usable form: identity systems, editorial models, environmental graphics, and digital interfaces — each developed against the principles set in Define.

Commit to three scheduled review gates with consistent attendees. Consolidate feedback into a single channel within 5 business days of each gate. Avoid mid-phase rescoping — additions land in a clearly logged change list for Phase 04.

Designs harden into production assets and shipping code. We work in two-week iterations with continuous QA, accessibility checks, and integration into the institution's content and infrastructure environment.

Provide hosting credentials and technical liaison by week one. Supply final copy and image rights at least two weeks before launch. Designate a UAT lead to coordinate testers and triage tickets through the staging period.

Launch is a coordinated event, not an upload. We sequence go-live with content, communications, and operational readiness so the institution's internal teams are equipped to run the new system from day one.

Free the internal team for two days of training during launch week. Coordinate cross-departmental announcements through your communications office. Plan for a moratorium on non-critical content changes for the first 10 days post-launch.

We close every engagement with a structured retrospective: what shipped, what shifted, what we learned, and what the institution should watch for in the months ahead. Debrief converts a project into institutional memory.

Attend the closing retrospective (half day) and ratify the 12-month roadmap. Nominate an internal steward to own the handover archive. Optional: agree a quarterly check-in cadence for the year following launch.
A side-by-side reading of how the six phases differ in cadence, team load, and risk. Use this as a scoping aid during early conversations — actual figures are tuned per engagement.
Most engagements modify two or three phases substantially. Tell us about the institution and timeline and we'll send back a scoped, phased proposal within ten working days. Start an enquiry →
A boutique brand strategy and digital experience agency for science, technology, and cultural institutions.

visibleText: Oblique Flux Studio · Est. 2011 Studio Work 01 Services 02 Studio 03 Process 04 Contact 05 Oblique Flux Studio Brand & experience for science, technology, and cultural institutions. NY · BER · MEL Method · 04 A six-phase engagement, calibrated for institutions. Every engagement at Oblique Flux moves through the same six phases — Discovery through Debrief — but their depth varies by context. Below is the durable structure we use to scope, contract, and operate research-led brand and product programs for museums, labs, universities, and public-sector clients. Discovery Define Design Develop Deploy Debrief Engagement note Phases run sequentially but overlap by roughly 15%. A full program typically spans 18–26 weeks; standalone audits can compress to 4. PHASE 01 Discovery 3–5 Weeks Research-led Stakeholder mapping Field immersion We begin by listening. The Discovery phase reconstructs the institution's recent decision history, audience mix, and operational constraints so subsequent strategy is grounded in evidence, not assumption. SI Stakeholder Interviews 12–20 conversations across leadership, curatorial, and operations. AA Audience Analysis Quant segmentation cross-referenced with on-site behaviour. CL Competitive Landscape Peer institutions, adjacent sectors, comparable digital products. HA Heritage Audit Archive, identity history, and prior strategic work review. Outputs Discovery report (40–60 pp.) Stakeholder map & quotes book Audience segment briefs Annotated peer landscape Client Responsibility Provide access to internal stakeholders for interviews (approx. 1 hour each), share prior research and brand artifacts, and nominate a single point-of-contact for scheduling. Plan for ~10 hours of total client time across the phase. PHASE 02 Define 2–3 Weeks Strategy Positioning Workshop We translate Discovery findings into a sharp strategic frame — the positioning, principles, and decision criteria that govern every downstream choice. Defined together, defended in writing. PS Positioning Statement A single paragraph that survives the next ten years of choices. BP Brand Principles 5–7 principles, each with a do/don't pair for designers. NA Narrative Arc Long-form story that powers admissions, fundraising, and PR. SC Success Criteria Measurable signals tied to mission and operations. Outputs Strategy brief Positioning & principles deck Messaging architecture Success-criteria matrix Client Responsibility Attend a two-day in-person definition workshop (or two half-day remote sessions) with 6–10 decision-makers. Designate a strategy approver empowered to ratify the positioning by the close of week three. PHASE 03 Design 5–8 Weeks Identity Editorial Wayfinding UI The Design phase resolves the strategic frame into visible, usable form: identity systems, editorial models, environmental graphics, and digital interfaces — each developed against the principles set in Define. ID Identity System Mark, type, color, motion logic, and tone-of-voice rules. CD Component Design Atomic UI library spec'd for the engineering handoff. EL Editorial Layouts Print and web templates anchored to a shared typographic scale. PR Prototype Reviews Three formal review gates with documented decisions. Outputs Identity guidelines (v1) UI component library Template set & specimens Interactive prototype Client Responsibility Commit to three scheduled review gates with consistent attendees. Consolidate feedback into a single channel within 5 business days of each gate. Avoid mid-phase rescoping — additions land in a clearly logged change list for Phase 04. PHASE 04 Develop 4–10 Weeks Build CMS QA Designs harden into production assets and shipping code. We work in two-week iterations with continuous QA, accessibility checks, and integration into the institution's content and infrastructure environment. FE Front-End Build Semantic, accessible markup paired to the component library. CM CMS Modeling Editor-friendly content types matched to editorial workflows. A11 Accessibility QA WCAG 2.2 AA pass with manual screen-reader verification. PA Production Assets Print-ready files, environmental specs, motion masters. Outputs Staging build & release notes CMS schema & editor docs Accessibility audit report Print & environmental files Client Responsibility Provide hosting credentials and technical liaison by week one. Supply final copy and image rights at least two weeks before launch. Designate a UAT lead to coordinate testers and triage tickets through the staging period. PHASE 05 Deploy 1–2 Weeks Launch Rollout Training Launch is a coordinated event, not an upload. We sequence go-live with content, communications, and operational readiness so the institution's internal teams are equipped to run the new system from day one. RP Rollout Plan Timed cutover sequence with rollback contingency. TR Team Training Two sessions for editors, designers, and comms staff. LC Launch Comms Internal memo kit and external press-ready materials. MN Monitoring Two-week stabilization window with daily check-ins. Outputs Production launch Training recordings & manuals Launch communications kit Stabilization log Client Responsibility Free the internal team for two days of training during launch week. Coordinate cross-departmental announcements through your communications office. Plan for a moratorium on non-critical content changes for the first 10 days post-launch. PHASE 06 Debrief 2 Weeks Measurement Retrospective Handover We close every engagement with a structured retrospective: what shipped, what shifted, what we learned, and what the institution should watch for in the months ahead. Debrief converts a project into institutional memory. PM Performance Metrics Baseline vs. post-launch readings against Phase 02 criteria. RE Retrospective Joint review with the working group, written and signed. RM Roadmap 12-month optimization plan with owners and budgets. HO Handover Pack Source files, repositories, contacts, license registry. Outputs Post-launch report Retrospective document 12-month roadmap Complete handover archive Client Responsibility Attend the closing retrospective (half day) and ratify the 12-month roadmap. Nominate an internal steward to own the handover archive. Optional: agree a quarterly check-in cadence for the year following launch. Use this as a scoping aid during early conversations — actual figures are tuned per engagement.
